content:
# Exodus Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32r–32v (PDF pp. 62–63); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 42–43 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_002_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_002_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_002_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_002_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And a man from the house of Levi went and took a daughter of Levi.

**2** And the woman conceived and bore a son; and she saw him, that he [was] good, and she hid him three months.

**3** And she was not able to hide him any longer, and she took for him a tevah [basket-ark] of papyrus-reeds and she smeared it with bitumen and with pitch; and she put the child in it and put [it] among the reeds at the lip of the Ye'or.

**4** And his sister stationed-herself from afar to know what would be done to him.

**5** And the daughter of Par'oh went down to bathe at the Ye'or, and her maidens [were] walking along the side of the Ye'or; and she saw the tevah [basket-ark] in the midst of the reeds, and she sent her handmaid and she took it.

**6** And she opened [it] and she saw him -- the child -- and behold, a boy weeping; and she had-compassion on him and she said: "From the children of the Hebrews [is] this-one."

**7** And his sister said to the daughter of Par'oh: "Shall I go and call for you a nursing woman from the Hebrew-women, that she may nurse the child for you?"

**8** And the daughter of Par'oh said to her: "Go." And the young-woman went and called the mother of the child.

**9** And the daughter of Par'oh said to her: "Cause to go this child and nurse him for me, and I will give your wages." And the woman took the child and nursed him.

**10** And the child grew, and she brought him to the daughter of Par'oh, and he became to her as a son; and she called his name Mosheh and said: "Because from the water I drew-him-out."

**11** And it was in those days, and Mosheh grew-up and he went out to his brothers and he looked upon their burdens; and he saw a Mitsri man striking a Hebrew man from his brothers.

**12** And he turned this-way and that-way and he saw that there-was-no man; and he struck the Mitsri and hid him in the sand.

**13** And he went out on the second day, and behold, two Hebrew men [were] struggling-together; and he said to the wicked-one: "Why do you strike your companion?"

**14** And he said: "Who set you [as] a man, a prince and a judge, over us? [Is it] to kill me you are saying [you intend], as you killed the Mitsri?" And Mosheh feared and said: "Surely the matter has become known."

**15** And Par'oh heard this matter and he sought to kill Mosheh; and Mosheh fled from the face of Par'oh and he dwelt in the land of Midyan and he sat upon the well.

**16** And the priest of Midyan [had] seven daughters; and they came and drew-[water] and filled the troughs to water the flock of their father.

**17** And the shepherds came and drove-them-away; and Mosheh arose and saved them and watered their flock.

**18** And they came to Re'u'el their father; and he said: "Why have you hastened to come today?"

**19** And they said: "A Mitsri man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds, and also drawing he drew for us and watered the flock."

**20** And he said to his daughters: "And where [is] he? Why [is] this [that] you have left the man? Call him, and let him eat bread."

**21** And Mosheh was content to dwell with the man; and he gave Tsipporah his daughter to Mosheh.

**22** And she bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, for he said: "A ger [sojourner/stranger] I have been in a foreign land."

**23** And it was in those many days, and the king of Mitsrayim died; and the sons of Yisra'el groaned from the labor and they cried-out; and their cry-for-help went up to God from the labor.

**24** And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Avraham, with Yitshaq, and with Ya'aqov.

**25** And God saw the sons of Yisra'el, and God knew.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A)

1. **v.1 -- "a man from the house of Levi":** The MT does not name the parents here. From Exodus 6:20 we know they are Amram and Yokheved, but the Leningrad Codex text at this point leaves them anonymous. The LXX (Vaticanus p. 42) follows the same anonymous construction: tis ek tes phules Leui ("someone from the tribe of Levi"). The LXX uses phule ("tribe") where the MT has bayit ("house").

2. **v.2 -- "that he was good" (ki tov hu):** The phrase ki tov deliberately echoes the creation refrain of Genesis 1 ("and God saw that [it was] good"). The mother sees the child as tov -- the same word God uses to evaluate his creation. The LXX renders asteion ("fine, well-formed"), which Hebrews 11:23 and Acts 7:20 later echo as asteios to theo ("beautiful before God").

3. **v.3 -- tevah:** This word occurs in only two contexts in the Hebrew Bible: here and in the Noah narrative (Genesis 6-9). It is the same word for Noah's ark. This is no coincidence -- both are vessels of deliverance upon water. The word is likely borrowed from Egyptian *dbt* (chest, coffin). I transliterate it and note the connection. The LXX uses thibin, a transliteration from the same Egyptian root, rather than the more common kiboton used for Noah's ark in Genesis LXX.

4. **v.3 -- "papyrus-reeds" (gome'):** The MT reads tevat gome', a basket made of papyrus. Papyrus was readily available along the Nile and naturally water-resistant. The LXX reads thibin helinen ("papyrus basket"), confirming the material.

5. **v.3 -- "bitumen and pitch" (ba-hemar u-va-zafet):** The waterproofing materials. Hemar is bitumen/asphalt; zafet is pitch/tar. The same hemar appears in Genesis 11:3 (Tower of Babel) and Genesis 14:10 (the tar pits of the Siddim valley). The LXX reads asphaltopiissei ("with bitumen-pitch"), combining both materials into a single compound term.

6. **v.3 -- "at the lip of the Ye'or" (al sefat ha-ye'or):** Literally "upon the lip of the Ye'or." Hebrew safah means both "lip" (of a mouth) and "edge/bank" (of a river). I preserve "lip" as the literal body-metaphor the codex employs.

7. **v.5 -- "her handmaid" (et amatah):** The MT reads amatah, which can mean either "her handmaid/maidservant" or "her arm" (ammah with different pointing). The Masoretic pointing in the Leningrad Codex supports "her handmaid" (amatah). However, the Septuagint reads ten therapainan autes ("her attendant"), supporting the "handmaid" reading. Some rabbinic traditions (BT Sotah 12b) read "her arm," suggesting a miraculous extension. The EMET translation follows the Masoretic pointing.

8. **v.6 -- "a boy weeping" (na'ar bokheh):** The word na'ar ("boy/youth") is used for the infant, which is unusual -- na'ar typically refers to an older child or young man. Some ancient versions harmonize: the LXX reads paidion klaion ("little child weeping"). The MT's use of na'ar for a newborn may foreshadow his future stature.

9. **v.10 -- Mosheh:** The name is explained with a Hebrew folk-etymology: "because from the water meshitihu (I drew him out)." The verb m-sh-h means "to draw out." However, the name Mosheh is morphologically an active participle ("one who draws out"), not a passive one ("one drawn out"). The Egyptian derivation -- from Egyptian *mose/mesu* ("born of, child of"), as in Thutmose, Rameses -- is likely the actual origin. The Hebrew text offers an Israelite reinterpretation. The LXX preserves the same etymology: Mousen, ek tou hudatos auton aneilomen ("from the water I took him up").

10. **v.11 -- "and Mosheh grew up" (va-yigdal Mosheh):** The same root g-d-l appears in v. 10 ("the child grew") and here again. In v. 10 it refers to physical growth as an infant; here it signals maturity to adulthood. The LXX distinguishes the two: v. 10 uses hadrunthe ("grew sturdy") and v. 11 uses megas genomenos ("having become great/adult").

11. **v.12 -- "and he turned this-way and that-way" (va-yifen koh va-koh):** The phrase shows Mosheh's deliberation before the act. He scans in both directions before striking. The LXX renders periblepsomenos hode kai hode ("having looked around this way and that"), preserving the same visual scanning.

12. **v.14 -- "a man, a prince and a judge" (le-'ish sar ve-shofet):** The Hebrew piles up three terms: man, prince/chief, judge. The accuser challenges Mosheh's self-appointed triple authority. The LXX reads archonta kai dikasten ("ruler and judge"), omitting "man" -- the same phrase that appears in Acts 7:27,35 when Stephen retells this episode.

13. **v.15 -- "and he sat upon the well" (va-yeshev al ha-be'er):** This scene-type echoes the betrothal-at-the-well motif: Avraham's servant for Rivqah (Gen 24), Ya'aqov and Rahel (Gen 29). Mosheh sits at the well and soon meets his future wife. The literary pattern is deliberate.

14. **v.18 -- Re'u'el:** The father-in-law is called Re'u'el here (meaning "friend/shepherd of God") but Yitro in Exodus 3:1 and elsewhere. The tradition preserves multiple names: Re'u'el, Yitro, Hovav (Judges 4:11). Some scholars take Re'u'el as a clan name and Yitro as a personal name. The Leningrad Codex reads re'u'el clearly. The LXX reads Ragouel.

15. **v.19 -- "A Mitsri man":** The daughters identify Mosheh as an Egyptian -- he apparently looked and dressed as one, having been raised in Par'oh's court. This detail preserves Mosheh's ambiguous identity: Hebrew by birth, Egyptian by appearance.

16. **v.22 -- Gershom:** The name is parsed as ger sham ("stranger there") or from the root g-r-sh ("to drive out/expel"). The MT provides the etymology: "a ger I have been in a foreign land" (ger hayiti be-erets nokhriyah). The LXX renders paroikos eimi en ge allotria ("I am a sojourner in a foreign land"), closely matching the Hebrew.

17. **v.23 -- "and the king of Mitsrayim died":** The MT does not name this Pharaoh. The Leningrad Codex reads simply va-yamot melekh Mitsrayim. The death marks a narrative turning point -- the sons of Yisra'el now cry out, and God responds.

18. **v.25 -- "and God knew" (va-yeda' Elohim):** The verse ends abruptly with no stated object: "and God knew." The verb y-d-' (to know) is left absolute -- God knew. Some translations supply an object ("[their condition]"), but the MT in the Leningrad Codex has no object. The LXX adds kai egnosthe autois ("and he was made known to them" / "and he acknowledged them"), supplying what the Hebrew deliberately leaves unsaid. The EMET translation preserves the stark, objectless "and God knew," honoring the codex's silence.

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary

The Vaticanus manuscript (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 42-43) preserves the Exodus 2 text in continuous Greek uncial script across three columns. Key divergences from the MT include: the use of thibin (transliterated from Egyptian) for the basket rather than kiboton (v. 3); the reading of paidion ("little child") for MT's na'ar ("boy") in v. 6; the omission of the triple designation "man, prince, and judge" in favor of "ruler and judge" in v. 14; and the significant expansion in v. 25 where LXX adds "and he was made known to them" against the MT's bare "and God knew." The chapter division visible on Vaticanus p. 43 shows the transition into Exodus 3 (the burning bush), with chapter notation (CAP. XLVIII visible at top of p. 43 in the Vaticanus numbering system). Overall, the LXX of Exodus 2 tracks the Hebrew closely in narrative sequence while exhibiting characteristic clarifying expansions.

— EMET
